# Project Charter

<!-- Generated by `spec-kitty charter generate` -->

Generated: 2026-04-27T04:26:37Z

## Testing Standards

- Every package ships with unit tests in its own `tests/` directory.
Integration tests cover cross-package boundaries. Each subsystem provides
in-memory implementations of its key interfaces so consuming packages can
test against them without booting a real database or full kernel.

CI testing matrix (mandatory): tests must run on PHP 8.4 across both
Ubuntu LTS and Debian stable, with SQLite and PostgreSQL as mandatory
database backends. MySQL is optional but encouraged. The full matrix is
declared in the canonical CI template at
`.github/workflows/ci-template.yml`. Flaky tests must be fixed at root
cause; CI re-runs are capped at one retry. A test that needs repeated
retries to pass is a charter violation, not an annoyance.

Benchmark tests asserting performance budgets (e.g., entity reflection
under 5 ms, cached lookups under 0.1 ms) are required where NFRs warrant
them and run via the canonical benchmark harness at `tools/bench/` (see
Performance Targets). Tests are part of the contract of every Spec Kitty
work package — no WP merges without its tests passing.


## Quality Gates

- Mandatory before merge (all enforced by CI; any CI job failure blocks
merge):
  - Full PHPUnit suite green across the mandatory CI matrix (PHP 8.4 ×
    Ubuntu/Debian × SQLite/Postgres). No skipped tests without
    justification.
  - PHPStan analysis clean against the committed baseline; new violations
    are fixed or explicitly added to the baseline with reviewer
    acknowledgement.
  - `bin/check-composer-policy` passes (no wildcard internal `waaseyaa/*`
    constraints, `@dev` only in root composer.json, `config.sort-packages`
    true on all first-party manifests).
  - SCA: `composer audit` (or equivalent) runs on every PR and the build
    fails on any high or critical advisory unless an active
    charter-exception issue covers the finding with a removal date.
  - Spec Kitty work-package framing for any non-trivial change. Direct
    commits to main without a mission are discouraged for substantive
    feature work but allowed for typos, infrastructure tweaks, and
    documentation-only changes.
  - `docs/specs/<subsystem>.md` updated in the same PR when a subsystem's
    contract changes (the orchestration table in CLAUDE.md identifies
    which spec each file pattern maps to).
  - CHANGELOG.md entry for any breaking change. New deprecations (post
    beta entry) listed under a `### Deprecated` heading in the release.
  - License headers and SPDX-License-Identifier preserved on every file
    (`GPL-2.0-or-later`).

CI enforcement: the repository exposes a canonical reusable workflow at
`.github/workflows/ci-template.yml`. Package- and feature-level
workflows must include or inherit it via `workflow_call`. Overriding or
bypassing the template requires a documented, timeboxed
charter-exception issue.


## Performance Benchmarks

- Performance is treated as a contract with explicit thresholds, not as
"fast enough." Where an NFR or benchmark exists, it must be asserted by a
test or codified in a baseline. Examples:
  - Entity reflection: under 5 ms for first call, under 0.1 ms cached.
  - Schema generation from entity types: bounded by the size of the
    registered entity catalog, no quadratic scans.
  - Test suite: full suite under 60 seconds locally where feasible.
Performance regressions surfaced by benchmarks block merge until
investigated. The framework prefers honest budgets over aspirational
ones — a 5 ms budget is a real commitment, not marketing.

Benchmark harness and baseline: the canonical benchmark harness lives
under `tools/bench/` and is invoked by CI via `tools/bench/run.sh`. It
runs in a pinned Docker image with fixed PHP runtime settings (opcache
enabled, JIT off unless an experiment flag is set, `memory_limit=256M`,
`realpath_cache_size=4M`). The pin is recorded in
`tools/bench/runtime.lock`. CI records benchmark results per merge to
`main` and stores baselines under `tools/bench/baselines/` keyed by
short-SHA. A regression of more than 10% relative to the previous
baseline (or beyond per-benchmark custom thresholds documented in
`tools/bench/README.md`) opens a blocking ticket tagged
`benchmark-regression`.


## Branch Strategy

- During alpha (current state), the governance model is solo-author with
AI-driven review via the Spec Kitty implement-review loop. The author
opens a Spec Kitty mission, AI agents implement and review work packages,
rejection cycles surface issues, and the author makes the final merge
decision. A second human reviewer is not required for alpha.

Mission and PR traceability is enforced — every PR must link to its Spec
Kitty mission and any associated GitHub issue (used as a tracker only,
not as the source of governance truth).

Beta entry criteria: to transition the framework from alpha to beta, all
of the following must be true and explicitly recorded in a
`release/v0.X-beta.md` decision note:
  1. Public-API freeze: a 14-day window during which no symbol in the
     defined Public API surface is added, removed, or has its signature
     changed. Exceptions during the freeze require an explicit charter
     amendment.
  2. Maintainer sign-off: at least two named maintainers approve the
     beta tag for every package in the `core` set (waaseyaa/foundation,
     waaseyaa/entity, waaseyaa/entity-storage, waaseyaa/field,
     waaseyaa/access, waaseyaa/routing, waaseyaa/api, waaseyaa/cli).
  3. CI green streak: 14 consecutive days of green CI runs on `main`
     across the full mandatory matrix. Any red run inside the window
     resets the streak.

At beta and beyond, public-API changes require explicit author approval
after AI review, and a later milestone introduces a two-author rule for
core packages.

- Deployment constraints: The framework is delivered as Composer packages on Packagist. Releases
are coordinated through `scripts/release.sh` (semver-tagged on the
monorepo root) and `splitsh-lite` (per-package read-only mirror repos).
Each package on Packagist is a split mirror; the monorepo root is the
development source. New packages must be split-published and registered
on Packagist before any released package can declare a transitive
dependency on them (the WP Mar 2025 oidc submission was the canonical
example). CI handles split + tag publishing; manual splits are an
escape hatch, not the default.

Releases must be signed (GPG or Sigstore-equivalent). Release automation
must publish checksums and signatures alongside the Packagist publish
step. The release playbook (see Documentation Policy → `RELEASE.md`)
enumerates the signing flow, checksum publication, and rollback
procedure.

Apps consuming Waaseyaa are expected to manage their own infrastructure
(database, queue, cache backends) and supply concrete bindings via
service providers; the framework ships interfaces and in-memory or SQL
defaults but takes no opinion on production deployment topology.


## Governance Activation

```yaml
mission: software-dev
selected_paradigms: [domain-driven-design]
selected_directives: []
available_tools: [git, spec-kitty]
template_set: software-dev-default
```

## Policy Summary

- Intent: Waaseyaa is a modern, entity-first, AI-native PHP content management
framework targeting PHP 8.4+. It is a clean-room replacement for Drupal's
runtime: takes the conceptual lessons (entity model, fieldable content,
pluggable storage) but ships none of the legacy code, none of the global
state, and no compatibility shims. The framework is delivered as a
Composer monorepo of independent packages organized in seven architectural
layers (Foundation → Core Data → Content Types → Services → API → AI →
Interfaces). Each package has explicit interfaces and is independently
testable. AI-first means the framework's own metadata (entity schemas,
field types, route definitions) is automatically discoverable and
actionable by AI agents — MCP tools generate from entity definitions,
schema flows into authoring assistance, and vector indexing is a
first-class capability rather than a bolt-on.

- Languages/Frameworks: PHP 8.4+ as the runtime baseline (no support for older versions). Symfony
7 components (HttpFoundation, Console, Routing, DependencyInjection,
Validator, etc.) as the foundation libraries. Doctrine DBAL (in-flight via
the doctrine-dbal-cutover mission) for the database layer. Twig for SSR.
Vue 3 + TypeScript + Nuxt 3 for the admin SPA. PHPUnit 11 for tests.
PHPStan for static analysis. Composer 2.x for package management. Spec
Kitty for governance and mission management.

- Testing: Every package ships with unit tests in its own `tests/` directory.
Integration tests cover cross-package boundaries. Each subsystem provides
in-memory implementations of its key interfaces so consuming packages can
test against them without booting a real database or full kernel.

CI testing matrix (mandatory): tests must run on PHP 8.4 across both
Ubuntu LTS and Debian stable, with SQLite and PostgreSQL as mandatory
database backends. MySQL is optional but encouraged. The full matrix is
declared in the canonical CI template at
`.github/workflows/ci-template.yml`. Flaky tests must be fixed at root
cause; CI re-runs are capped at one retry. A test that needs repeated
retries to pass is a charter violation, not an annoyance.

Benchmark tests asserting performance budgets (e.g., entity reflection
under 5 ms, cached lookups under 0.1 ms) are required where NFRs warrant
them and run via the canonical benchmark harness at `tools/bench/` (see
Performance Targets). Tests are part of the contract of every Spec Kitty
work package — no WP merges without its tests passing.

- Quality Gates: Mandatory before merge (all enforced by CI; any CI job failure blocks
merge):
  - Full PHPUnit suite green across the mandatory CI matrix (PHP 8.4 ×
    Ubuntu/Debian × SQLite/Postgres). No skipped tests without
    justification.
  - PHPStan analysis clean against the committed baseline; new violations
    are fixed or explicitly added to the baseline with reviewer
    acknowledgement.
  - `bin/check-composer-policy` passes (no wildcard internal `waaseyaa/*`
    constraints, `@dev` only in root composer.json, `config.sort-packages`
    true on all first-party manifests).
  - SCA: `composer audit` (or equivalent) runs on every PR and the build
    fails on any high or critical advisory unless an active
    charter-exception issue covers the finding with a removal date.
  - Spec Kitty work-package framing for any non-trivial change. Direct
    commits to main without a mission are discouraged for substantive
    feature work but allowed for typos, infrastructure tweaks, and
    documentation-only changes.
  - `docs/specs/<subsystem>.md` updated in the same PR when a subsystem's
    contract changes (the orchestration table in CLAUDE.md identifies
    which spec each file pattern maps to).
  - CHANGELOG.md entry for any breaking change. New deprecations (post
    beta entry) listed under a `### Deprecated` heading in the release.
  - License headers and SPDX-License-Identifier preserved on every file
    (`GPL-2.0-or-later`).

CI enforcement: the repository exposes a canonical reusable workflow at
`.github/workflows/ci-template.yml`. Package- and feature-level
workflows must include or inherit it via `workflow_call`. Overriding or
bypassing the template requires a documented, timeboxed
charter-exception issue.

- Review Policy: During alpha (current state), the governance model is solo-author with
AI-driven review via the Spec Kitty implement-review loop. The author
opens a Spec Kitty mission, AI agents implement and review work packages,
rejection cycles surface issues, and the author makes the final merge
decision. A second human reviewer is not required for alpha.

Mission and PR traceability is enforced — every PR must link to its Spec

not as the source of governance truth).

Beta entry criteria: to transition the framework from alpha to beta, all

  1. Public-API freeze: a 14-day window during which no symbol in the

  2. Maintainer sign-off: at least two named maintainers approve the

At beta and beyond, public-API changes require explicit author approval

  - Test suite: full suite under 60 seconds locally where feasible.

investigated. The framework prefers honest budgets over aspirational
ones — a 5 ms budget is a real commitment, not marketing.

are coordinated through `scripts/release.sh` (semver-tagged on the

Each package on Packagist is a split mirror; the monorepo root is the

on Packagist before any released package can declare a transitive

## Project Directives

1. Respect risk boundaries: Absolute non-negotiables:
  - No re-introduction of any Drupal-runtime dependency or
    Drupal-shaped global state. The `waaseyaa/database-legacy` package
    exists explicitly as a transitional bridge (named "legacy" to
    flag its temporary nature) and is being replaced by Doctrine DBAL.
  - No bypassing of `bin/check-composer-policy`, the layered architecture
    rules, or the access-control semantics codified in `docs/specs/`.
  - No registration of services or entity types outside the documented
    provider mechanism. No global `$GLOBALS`, no service locators
    masquerading as DI containers.
  - No silent breaking changes to public PHP API. Breaking changes are
    explicit, called out in CHANGELOG.md, and accompanied by an
    UPGRADING.md migration recipe.
  - No skipping of git hooks (--no-verify) or signing bypasses without
    explicit justification.
  - No committing of secrets, credentials, or generated artifacts to the
    repository.

Public-API removal policy is phase-dependent. During alpha, see DIR-003
(Greenfield Removal Policy). At beta entry and beyond, removals from
the public API surface follow formal deprecation:
  - Use the `@deprecated <since-version> <reason and migration target>`
    PHPDoc tag at the symbol's declaration site.
  - Reference a target removal version (e.g., "since 1.2.0 — remove
    in 2.0.0") and a one-line migration note pointing at the replacement
    symbol or recipe.
  - Remain in place for at least two minor releases unless the symbol
    is security-critical, in which case removal cadence follows the
    security-advisory timeline.
  - Are listed under a `### Deprecated` heading in `CHANGELOG.md` for
    the release that introduces them.

2. Keep documentation synchronized with workflow and behavior changes.

3. Greenfield Removal Policy: during alpha (current state), the
greenfield principle applies. When a better pattern lands, the old one
is removed outright. No deprecation window is required. Backwards-compat
shims that retain known-bad patterns are forbidden. `@deprecated`
wrappers, `Legacy*` namespaces, parallel `v2` interfaces, and "for
backward compatibility" comments are not acceptable substitutes for
deletion. Architecture quality is preferred over API stability for the
duration of alpha. Breaking changes are still announced explicitly per
DIR-001 (CHANGELOG.md entry, UPGRADING.md migration recipe) —
communication discipline is preserved; compatibility debt is not.
Severity is policy-binding regardless of the `severity: warn` field in
`directives.yaml` (Spec Kitty 3.1.6 hardcodes severity for all
directives synced from `charter.md`); the binding force of this
directive comes from its text.

## Reference Index

| Reference ID | Kind | Summary | Local Doc |
|---|---|---|---|
| `USER:PROJECT_PROFILE` | user_profile | Project-specific interview answers captured for charter compilation. | `_LIBRARY/user-project-profile.md` |
| `PARADIGM:domain-driven-design` | paradigm | Software design guided by a deep model of the business domain. Strategic design decomposes a system into Bounded Contexts connected through explicit Context Mapping patterns (ACL, OHS, Shared Kernel, Published Language, etc.). Within each context a Ubiquitous Language keeps code, model, and domain expert communication aligned. Tactical patterns (Aggregates, Entities, Value Objects, Domain Events, Repositories, Domain Services) enforce invariants at the model boundary. DDD treats the domain model as the primary artifact — code is a direct expression of that model, not a translation layer above a database schema or UI framework.
 | `_LIBRARY/paradigm-domain-driven-design.md` |
| `TEMPLATE_SET:software-dev-default` | template_set | Build high-quality software with structured workflows and test-driven development | `_LIBRARY/template-set-software-dev-default.md` |

## Amendment Process

Charter amendments follow the Spec Kitty mission flow: open a mission
targeting `.kittify/charter/`, write a spec describing the policy change
and rationale, generate a new charter via `spec-kitty charter generate
--from-interview --force`, and merge through the standard implement /
review / accept loop. Amendments that tighten constraints (e.g., adding
a mandatory quality gate) take effect on the next mission. Amendments
that loosen constraints (e.g., relaxing a performance budget) require
explicit author rationale citing operational evidence. The charter is
not a snapshot — it is expected to evolve as the framework matures from
alpha through v1.0 and beyond.


## Exception Policy

Genuine exceptions to charter rules are rare and are documented inline
where they apply (e.g., a `// charter-exception: <issue#> <rationale>`
comment near the offending code). Exceptions must:
  1. Have a tracked GitHub issue with the `charter-exception` label.
  2. Include the following metadata in the issue body (machine-parsable):
     `removal_date: YYYY-MM-DD` (hard expiry date), `scope:` (paths or
     identifiers covered), `rationale:` (one paragraph explaining why
     the deviation exists).
  3. Be visible in code review and not buried in a comment thread.

Tracking automation: the canonical issue template at
`.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/charter-exception.md` enforces the metadata
shape. A scheduled job at `.github/workflows/charter-exceptions.yml`
runs weekly, lists active exceptions, posts a tracking summary, and
auto-escalates exceptions whose `removal_date` has passed (adds the
`charter-exception-overdue` label and re-pings the assignee). Two
consecutive overdue cycles trigger maintainer review.

Routine deviations from the charter (skipping a quality gate, breaking
public API silently, etc.) are not exceptions — they are charter
violations and the PR is rejected.
